title: Compound Interest: Mr. Riyuzaki and Mr. Yagami are having an argument on...
summary: Compare effective annual rates across accounts with different compounding frequencies.
prompt:
Mr. Riyuzaki and Mr. Yagami are having an argument on which bank offers the
best saving accounts. In fact, Mr. Riyuzaki believes that his bank offers a better
package, and he deposits his money in a Bank of Osaka account which offers
7.3488% compounded 12 time(s) per year. On the other hand, Mr. Yagami deposits
his money in a Bank of Okinawa account which offers $38,937 compounded 15
time(s) per year. Is Mr. Riyuzaki right? That is, which bank offers the best savings
account, and by how much?
correct_option:
Mr. Riyuzaki's wrong, since his bank offers an effective annual rate of 5.7145%
which is smaller than Mr. Yagami's effective annual rate of 7.0941%. The former is
smaller by 1.256%
answer_text:
Mr. Riyuzaki's wrong, since his bank offers an effective annual rate of 5.7145%
which is smaller than Mr. Yagami's effective annual rate of 7.0941%. The former is
smaller by 1.256%
solution:
Step 1 Highlight Relevant Information & Define Key Variables
Mr. Riyuzaki and Mr. Yagami are having an argument on which bank offers the
best saving accounts. In fact, Mr. Riyuzaki believes that his bank offers a better
package, and he deposits his money in a Bank of Osaka account which offers
5.7145% compounded 4 time(s) per year. On the other hand, Mr. Yagami
deposits his money in a Bank of Okinawa account which offers 6.8733%
compounded 12 time(s) per year. Is Mr. Riyuzaki right? That is, which bank offers
the best savings account, and by how much?
Generating a comparison between both Quoted Rates would be akin to a
comparison of apples and oranges. Both rates are compounded at a different
frequency, therefore, it wouldn't make sense to compare 5.7145% with 6.8733%
without setting them to their Effective Annual Rate EAR.
The formula to set Quoted Rates to Effective Annual Rates can be written as
follows
EAR1QRm)m−1EAR \left 1\frac{QR}{m} \right )^{m}-1EAR1+mQR)m−1
QROsaka: 5.7145%
mOsaka: 4
QROkinawa: 6.8733%
mOkinawa: 12
Step 2 Visualizing Quoted Rates & Effective Rates
Although you aren't requested to do this on an exam, it may be relevant to
visualize what a Quoted Rate and it's number of compounding periods actually
Calculator studio solutions 19
represents. Here's a graphic step-by-step guide on Quoted Rates, Effective Rates,
and the Compound Interest being built throughout that process
Timeline for Mr. Riyuzaki's Bank
A few things to note...Notice how 5.7145% was divided by 4 and spread troughout
our timeline? This means that every 3 months 1.4286% will be compounded on
Mr. Riyuzaki's interest and principal.
Table
Let us assume that Mr. Riyuzaki was to invest $100 today in his Bank of Osaka
account. Here's how his money would grow if he were to earn 5.7145%
compounded 4 time(s) per year.
A few things to note:
The first row outlines all the calendar months (i.e., t=0 t=12
The seconds row represents the funds (re)invested by Mr. Riyuzaki; hence, the
inclusion of interest built in the previous month (i.e., period). Notice how the value
in this row will increase if compounded more than once per year
The third row represents the interest generated on the funds (re)invested by Mr.
Riyuzaki. Notice how the interest generated grows every 3 month(s); hence, the
value of compound interest
The fourth row represents the sum of the funds (re)invested by Mr. Riyuzaki and
the interest generated on the funds (re)invested
Therefore, at the end of the year, Mr. Riyuzaki would have generated $5.84
interest on his initial $100 investment. Therefore, his return on investment (i.e., P1
P0/P0 would be 5.8382%. Notice how that value is larger than 5.7145%? It should
be obvious now! His initial investment had the chance to accrue interest 4 times
per year (i.e., being compounded every 3 months) instead of simply compounding
interest once per year.
Step 3 Solving for Mr. Riyuzaki's Effective Annual Rate
The formula to set Quoted Rates to Effective Annual Rates can be written as
follows
EAR1QRm)m−1EAR \left 1\frac{QR}{m} \right )^{m}-1EAR1+mQR)m−1
QROsaka: 5.7145%
Calculator studio solutions 20
mOsaka: 4
Such that, EAROsaka 5.8382%
Step 4 Solving for Mr. Yagami's Effective Annual Rate
The formula to set Quoted Rates to Effective Annual Rates can be written as
follows
EAR1QRm)m−1EAR \left 1\frac{QR}{m} \right )^{m}-1EAR1+mQR)m−1
QROkinawa: 6.8733%
mOkinawa: 12
Such that, EAROkinawa 7.0941%
Step 5 So What?
Mr. Riyuzaki's wrong, since his bank offers an effective annual rate of 5.7145%
which is smaller than Mr. Yagami's effective annual rate of 7.0941%. The former is
smaller by 1.256%
